17 min read
Welcome to The Trafпњљc Engine. I am grateful, and honored, that
youвАЩre here.
IвАЩm going to jump right in with an idea from Switch by Dan and
Chip Heath. The subtitle of that book is вАЬHow to Change Things
When Change is HardвАЭ, and itвАЩs an excellent compilation of well-
researched strategies for making positive change.
Switch introduces a concept called a вАЬdestination postcardвАЭ вАФ a
вАЬvivid picture from the near-term future that shows what could be
possible.вАЭ
Module 0 is my attempt at creating a destination postcard for The
Trafпњљc Engine. I want you to see what you can create in our next
eight weeks together (and beyond).
There are two broad paths you might take and I want to address
both.
Before I do that, however, I want to deпњљne a critically important
term that IвАЩll be using a lot in this training вАФ oпњљer.
An offer is the marketing expression of a product or service at a
Module 0 вАФ The 30,000вА≤ 	View

-- 1 of 17 --

speciпњљc point in time. It is not the product or service itself.
For example, The Trafпњљc Engine is a product. The discounted price
and live interaction with me for the пњљrst cohort for a speciпњљc
period of time (April 1 вАФ 7, 2020) was an offer.
When The Trafпњљc Engine is released again in the future, itвАЩll be
the same product (with some revisions, of course), but the offer
will be different. The price will change, interaction with me will
change, etc.
One product can turn into any number of offers. Think about the
differences between The Trafпњљc Engine delivered online, the way
youвАЩre participating now, vs. delivered in person during a small-
group intensive вАШboot campвАЩ style event.
Those are two completely different offers with very different
requirements (and very different price points). However, the
product itself вАФ my externalized thoughts delivered in a way that
can be learned by others вАФ is the same.
LetвАЩs use an ofпњљine example to further explore the idea. One of my
favorite restaurants has a вАЬburger nightвАЭ on Tuesdays. Any other
day of the week burgers are $12, but on Tuesdays that same burger
is $6. Same product, different offer.
Why? Tuesdays are the slowest day of the week for that
restaurant. The low-priced offer is an attempt to get more

-- 2 of 17 --

customers in the door. Margins in the restaurant business are
slim, and IвАЩm guessing the $6 burger is break even to acquire a
customer. The proпњљt is made on the beers, appetizers, and
desserts.
This is an important concept to understand. Your products and
services are not your offers, but your offers depend on your
products and services (or experience and skills you would like to
turn into products or services).
Another important idea to understand is that the overwhelming
majority of offers fail. If you spend much time in direct response
circles, youвАЩll learn that approximately 80% of all offers fail.
Again вАФ the offers fail, not necessarily the products those offers
represent.
DonвАЩt be discouraged if your initial attempts fail too. ThatвАЩs all part
of the process. WeвАЩll discuss ways to test offers in advance with
minimal investment of time, energy, and dollars, and other ways
to stack the deck in your favor.
OK, now that we understand what I mean when I use the term
offer, letвАЩs talk about the two different paths you might be on in
The Trafпњљc Engine, and what each path will look like in the near
future if you do the work.
I say вАЬifвАЭ because I canвАЩt do the push ups for you, and part of the

-- 3 of 17 --

destination postcard involves you imagining yourself rolling up
your sleeves and doing the work. Trust me, itвАЩll be worth it.
Path #1 assumes you already have a customer-generating
offer (meaning people have paid you money for a product or
service you provide, or an afпњљliate offer you promote). You might
be getting trafпњљc from organic sources, word of mouth, afпњљliates,
referrals, etc.
You may be buying trafпњљc already too.
If youвАЩre on this пњљrst path, and you do the work, by the end of
eight weeks you will have at least one highly-focused Google Ads
search campaign sending high-quality trafпњљc to your offer, and
you will be calculating cost-per-acquisition (CPA) and average
order value (AOV) for that campaign accurately.
You also will have at least one paid Facebook advertising
campaign sending trafпњљc to your offer, and youвАЩll be calculating
CPA and AOV for that as well.
In addition to these two prospecting campaigns, you will also
have a behaviorally-based retargeting strategy for both the
Google Display Network and Facebook. You will use qualitative
factors like time on site to eliminate low-quality trafпњљc, and focus
your retargeting ad spend on recapturing value from your
prospecting campaigns.

-- 4 of 17 --

Think of these retargeting campaigns as automated follow up. For
example, if someone sees your sales page for longer than 30
seconds but does not become a customer, s/he might see ads for a
lead magnet. That person may not have been ready to buy when
you were ready to sell, but s/he may be interested in becoming
part of your tribe.
ThatвАЩs just one example вАФ there are seemingly inпњљnite ways to
use retargeting well. Rather than make it complicated, weвАЩre going
to identify the highest value follow up (retargeting) campaigns to
create for different types of businesses and make sure youвАЩve done
the obvious before thinking about anything esoteric.
This is the minimum I would expect you to have created by the end
of the пњљrst eight weeks. The sky is the limit for whatвАЩs possible
beyond that.
One last thought about this пњљrst path through The Trafпњљc Engine.
I include lead generation in this пњљrst path as well, even if those
leads are not being monetized immediately. Even though lead
generation doesnвАЩt generate money initially, prospects are paying
with their attention and thatвАЩs an important consideration.
The challenge with lead generation is that we only see one side of
the пњљnancial equation (cost to acquire a lead вАФ CPL). We donвАЩt
know the rate at which those leads will convert to customers вАФ or
if they will at all вАФ until weвАЩve made an offer.

-- 5 of 17 --

Regardless вАФ lead generation is an important part of digital
marketing and I want to make sure we discuss that in detail.
For those people on this пњљrst path, after the initial eight weeks, I
expect that you will return to modules three through seven and
continue to add prospecting campaigns to scale trafпњљc
intelligently, always keeping an eye on CPA and AOV. Adding and
optimizing campaigns, and tracking the metrics that matter, will
become second nature to you over time.
More importantly, you are learning how to do this work, and how
to think about the principles and strategies behind the work
youвАЩre doing. Those are skills youвАЩll take with you and improve,
day by day, as you build new and better trafпњљc engines for your
business.
ThereвАЩs another path as well. Path #2 is for students who have
an idea to validate before creating an offer, or want to test new
ideas for an existing product or service before committing to
creating a new offer.
For those students, I suggest using the keyword and audience
research insights from modules three and four, setting up a
Google Ads account, but not creating a search campaign (yet).
There may be exceptions to this suggestion and IвАЩll make those
clear.
Instead, modules пњљve and six, and the Facebook Offer and

-- 6 of 17 --

Messaging Testing Framework masterclass, will explain how to
test offer ideas using long-copy Facebook ads.
That framework will show you how to identify the ideas that
resonate most with audiences, eliminate the ideas that donвАЩt, and
point the way toward future success.
Students on this path will validate an idea (or ideas) пњљrst, use that
information to create an offer, and then setup Google Ads and
Facebook prospecting campaigns, followed by Google and
Facebook retargeting campaigns.
Timing for this second group will depend on the results from
testing. A clear, positive signal early would speed up the process.
However, donвАЩt rush through this part. Use the opportunity to
gather these powerful insights and that investment of time,
energy, and dollars will pay dividends later.
These two paths are not mutually exclusive. Your situation may
beneпњљt from sending trafпњљc to an existing offer while also testing
ideas for a new offer or project. Consider my descriptions of these
two paths to be two ends of a spectrum of options, with your
individual needs somewhere in between.
I will be creating an offer along the way too, and I want to do that
in a way that is most useful and instructive for you. I explained
my approach in one of the emails for The Trafпњљc Engine. Those of
you who signed up early may have missed that, so IвАЩm including it

-- 7 of 17 --

here so youвАЩll know how I will be creating and testing my offer (or
offers).
Sun, April 5, 2020
IвАЩve been thinking about ideas for a real oпњљer to use as an example
during the implementation phase of The Traпњљc Engine (modules 3 вАФ 7).
Ideally, I want to demonstrate two diпњљerent approaches with the same
oпњљerвА¶
One will be paid traпњљc to an SOI-style front end followed by an ARM-
inspired email sequence that leads to a low-priced ($97) oпњљer.
The other version will be paid traпњљc straight to the same $97 oпњљer.
(No pressure, right?)
While thinking about this part of the course yesterday, it occurred to me
how easily I could make decisions вАФ with the best of intentions вАФ that
would make the course much worse for participants.
Let me explain what I mean, and how I intend to avoid those mistakes
(which may lead to a spectacularly entertaining disaster).
If youвАЩve ever watched a televised cooking show youвАЩll notice something
interesting. The host spends the majority of the show making a recipe
which is never actually cooked!

-- 8 of 17 --

Instead, the moment all the prep work is пњљnished, a beautiful, perfect
creation appears from the oven.
Ta-da!
The assumption is that the host did the exact same work for both recipes
and that the results would be the same.
I donвАЩt do a lot of cooking, but IвАЩm conпњљdent that my creation вАФ following
the exact steps the show host used вАФ wouldnвАЩt look like what I would
see on my TV screen.
Not. Even. Close.
Why?
The host has so many subtle advantages that are hard to communicate.
The tiny adjustments, better tools, пњљner ingredients, more consistent oven
temperatures вАФ all of those add up.
When the home audienceвАЩs creations donвАЩt match what they have seen on
TV, itвАЩs easy for them to think that itвАЩs somehow their fault.
And that is exactly what I want to avoid with The Traпњљc Engine.
Let me explain some ways I could create subtle advantages for the example
oпњљer, ever so slightly stacking the deck in my favor.
I could:

-- 9 of 17 --

вЦ™ Start working on (and testing ideas for) the oпњљer now to pre-validate
the right direction.
вЦ™ Leverage my experience and expertise which would add signiпњљcantly
to my oпњљerвАЩs credibility.
вЦ™ вАШWarm upвАЩ ad accounts and audiences.
вЦ™ Use data, strategies, models, or themes from client campaigns that
IвАЩve seen work well.
And thatвАЩs only a partial list.
It would be easy to make all of these choices with the best of intentions. I
could convince myself that I would be showing students strategies that
work best, ideas to implement, etc.
But, thereвАЩs a potential danger as well.
What if I donвАЩt fail on my пњљrst attempt and a student does?
What if I make it look easy, glossing over the fact that the пњљrst day sending
paid traпњљc to an untested oпњљer usually is the worst day?
If I were to do that, even with the best of intentions, I would be deceiving
students into thinking that they had done something wrong, or that they
were deпњљcient in some way.
That would be a terrible outcome.
To overcome these potential issues, hereвАЩs how I am approaching the oпњљer

-- 10 of 17 --

IвАЩll use as the implementation example for this пњљrst cohort of The Traпњљc
Engine.
I wonвАЩt start working on the oпњљer, in any capacity, until April 8th
when the course oпњљcially begins.
I wonвАЩt create an oпњљer related to my subject matter expertise in marketing,
paid digital advertising, or running a digital agency.
I wonвАЩt create ad accounts until I create those for the вАЬhow toвАЭ training,
and I wonвАЩt create any campaigns in either ad account that participants
arenвАЩt creating as well.
I wonвАЩt upload any data to create higher quality lookalike audiences or any
kind of warm audiences.
And, I wonвАЩt use any data, strategies, methods, or models from a high-
performing client.
A skeptic might think that this is all a stunt, anticipating excellent
performance and acting fake-humble when I manage to hit a home run.
The reality, however, is that doing this means I probably will
underperform on my пњљrst eпњљorts and thatвАЩs perfectly OK.
Once you understand that poor performance is just a part of the process вАФ
often itвАЩs the starting point вАФ and that day after day we iterate our
way to success, then you will have internalized the most important
lesson I can teach you.

-- 11 of 17 --

OK, letвАЩs move on and discuss a high-level overview of what weвАЩll
be doing and why.
And, in the spirit of full disclosure, this approach is much more natural for
meвА¶
In a previous email I mentioned that I believe (deeply) in the phrase, вАЬhow
you do anything is how you do everything.вАЭ
I do like to plan, but for the most part, my natural tendency is to jump out
of airplanes and try to create a parachute on my way to the ground.
Participants in The Traпњљc Engine are going to see me do that with the
implementation example oпњљer вАФ wish me luck!
My goal in taking this approach, aside from amusing many of you (and,
no doubt, Andr√©), is to show exactly what itвАЩs like to bring forth value into
the real world.
In general, itвАЩs not always sunshine and unicorns, and thatвАЩs OK.
When you see the reality of what to expect, and learn how to respond day
after day, you will understand what it really takes to succeed with paid
traпњљc.
I had a conversation with a dear friend recently and she reminded me of a
timeless truth I think youвАЩll пњљnd useful. More about that tomorrow.

-- 12 of 17 --

At a fundamental level, we will be monetizing attention. We will
be spending money to attract the right people into our worlds
with the intention of generating more money than we spent on
that initial investment.
Depending on our business model, that might mean breaking
even on initial sales and making our money on the back end over
time, or trying to be proпњљtable on the front end.
вАЬFront endвАЭ and вАЬback endвАЭ are two terms I need to deпњљne
clearly as well. The front end of your business is whatever you
sell that turns a prospect into a customer.
For example, a $97, 90-minute masterclass about one particular
part of the value you offer your customers could be a front end
product.
The critical thing to understand about front end offers is that they
convert prospects into customers (usually are at a lower price
point).
Back end offers are what you sell after someone has become a
customer. That could be a sophisticated value ladder of offers, or
simple a more expensive core offer.
A very common online example is a $97 front end offer that leads
to a $497 offer (which may lead to a $1,997 offer eventually). The
price points and speciпњљcs donвАЩt matter. What matters for our

-- 13 of 17 --

purposes is that front end offers acquire customers вАФ
hopefully at break even or slightly better вАФ and back end offers
generate the businessвАЩs proпњљts (usually over time).
Now that we have a basic understanding of front end vs. back end
offers, letвАЩs talk about a вАЬbreak evenвАЭ front end or вАЬgoing negativeвАЭ.
Breaking even on the front end means that the cost to acquire a
customer (CPA) is approximately the same as the money a
customer spends in his or her пњљrst interaction (average order
value, AOV).
For example: if it costs me $100 to acquire a customer, and I sell a
$100 front end product, I would be operating at break even (which
means IвАЩm getting customers for free).
For typical entrepreneurs this sounds crazy. What kind of
business spends $100 to acquire a customer who then only spends
$100? ThereвАЩs no proпњљt so how can this be a business? Proпњљt on
the front end is a very rare luxury вАФ if you can make that
work then wake up every day thanking your lucky stars.
Acquiring customers at break even is the Holy Grail of marketing.
When you do that youвАЩre getting customers for free, and those
customers will continue to buy from you over time as long as you
continue to create valuable offers (and treat your audience well).
вАЬGoing negativeвАЭ means spending more to acquire a customer

-- 14 of 17 --

than the money made in my пњљrst sale. For example, if I spend $120
to acquire a customer, and I sell a $100 product, itвАЩs costing me $20
to acquire a customer. The assumption in this example is that I
continue to sell over time and make my proпњљt on back end sales.
For example: if I own a supplement company and it costs me $75
to acquire a customer, that customer buys a $50 two-month trial
of my best-selling product, subscribes for auto-delivery of that
product for $50/month, and the average customer subscribes for
six months, вАЬgoing negativeвАЭ actually was proпњљtable.
How? LetвАЩs look at the numbers. $75 CPA вАФ $50 AOV for the пњљrst
sale means IвАЩm -$25 to acquire a customer. However, on average,
customers spend an additional $300 ($50/month x six months =
lifetime value вАФ LTV).
My gross revenue per customer would be + $275.
HereвАЩs the formula: AOV ($50) вАФ CPA ($75) + LTV ($300) = $275.
(DonвАЩt worry about understanding the metrics yet. We will talk about
those repeatedly throughout The Traпњљc Engine and youвАЩll internalize their
importance over time.)
And yes, I know that if you sell a $50 supplement there are costs to
produce that product, to run your business, etc. For now, letвАЩs
focus on the overall ideas and then reпњљne the speciпњљc applications
at the appropriate time.

-- 15 of 17 --

Something to keep in mind вАФ it is highly unlikely that weвАЩll be
proпњљtable immediately. I know the world is full of вАЩ10x trafпњљc
secretsвАЩ and other bold claims, but the reality is that the
economics of paid trafпњљc can be difпњљcult, especially at пњљrst.
Knowing that, weвАЩre going to develop the discipline of iterating
our way to proпњљtability one step at a time. You will have a
signiпњљcant head start because The Trafпњљc Engine is built on
timeless principles which have been translated into costly
mistakes to avoid, and high value activities to implement.
IвАЩve spent tens of thousands of hours learning what works and
what doesnвАЩt, and weвАЩre going to focus on those few things that
matter most.
The Trafпњљc Engine methodology is designed to give you a
signiпњљcant advantages over trial and error, and show you how to
create and then build upon success systematically.
We covered a lot of ground in this pre-training module. Now you
know the road ahead and what you can expect to create during
the next eight weeks and beyond.
IвАЩm glad to have you here, and I am so excited to see what you
create.
вАФ Shawn
